formulas.label: Electricity cost
formulas.example: 18,600 kWh/mo × €0.30 ≈ €5,580/mo (Wok To Walk Leidsestraat site data)

formulas.label: Vs baseline
formulas.example: 142 kWh today vs 220 kWh baseline → ▼ 35% — dashboard KPI chips use this for traffic-light scores

formulas.label: Site monthly → daily
formulas.example: Portfolio view scales site monthly totals when comparing Amsterdam HQ vs pilot sites

formulas.label: Deep dive line cost
formulas.example: One wok line: gas-heavy mix — sub-meter gas and electrical aux separately for accuracy

formulas.label: Upgrade payback
formulas.example: €8k net capex ÷ €220/mo savings ≈ 36 months — savings projection chart on Greenways

formulas.label: Cost leak detector
formulas.example: kWh above target profile × tariff floor → €/mo leak estimate on analytics row

timeOfUse.headline: When you use energy matters as much as how much
points: Dashboard 24h trend shows peak hours (often lunch service 11:00–14:00 for restaurants) — shifting prep or HVAC pre-cool to off-peak cuts €/kWh on dynamic tariffs.
Running identical equipment at peak vs off-peak can differ 2× on some business tariffs even if kWh is the same — Vincent covers tariff compare; Edwardo ties it to visible load shapes.
Refrigeration and standby loads running 24/7 flatten the curve — efficient ETL equipment lowers the baseline band, not just peak spikes.
Batch dishwash and combi-oven cycles timed away from peak electricity windows often save more than one extra efficient appliance bought but run at peak.
Gas wok lines dominate thermal cost — monitoring m³ separately from kWh avoids mis-attributing savings to the wrong fuel.
